1:
The witnesses gather at the birth of the Old Testament's most celebrated friendship, and they read the knitting of two souls with one voice while dividing gently over what kindled it. The grammatical-historical Ellicott does the close work on the Hebrew: the verb rendered knit is better bound up, a strong term used of Jacob's love for Benjamin, whose life 'is bound up in the lad's life' (Genesis 44:30), and he recalls Aristotle's saying that friends are called one soul. What won Jonathan, he insists, was not the shepherd's bravery or skill in the combat — the son of Saul had those himself — but David's sublime, childlike faith in the 'Glorious Arm' of the Lord; the two were typical Israelites, one in an intense confidence in the Mighty One of Israel. Adam Clarke, whose learning here is philological, notes that the whole opening was omitted by the Septuagint and crowns it with the Greek proverb that friendship 'is one soul in two bodies: a friend is another self.'

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the scene as providence at work. David was anointed to take the crown out of Saul's hand and over Jonathan's head, yet God so orders it that the reigning king confides in him and the heir covenants with him, that David's way might be the clearer when his rival was his friend. None had so much reason to dislike David as Jonathan, 'yet none regards him more'; and Henry marks the wonder that affections governed by wisdom and grace are not alienated by any secular consideration, for 'the greater thoughts will swallow up and overrule the less.' The reformed Gill adds that when David 'made an end of speaking' much more passed between them than is recorded, David expressing himself with such prudence and piety that Jonathan's soul was immediately knit to his.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley states the ground plainly: Jonathan loved him for his excellent virtues and endowments, for the service he had done to God and his people, and for the similitude of their age and qualities. The voices land together on the pastoral note Henry presses: this union of souls is the fruit of divine grace, which makes true believers of one heart and one soul; and he lifts the eyes higher still, for our Lord Jesus 'stripped himself to clothe us, emptied himself to enrich us,' doing more even than Jonathan, who clothed David without putting on David's rags.
2:
This short verse marks a turning point the witnesses are careful not to hurry past: from this day David belongs to the court and no longer to the sheepfold. Keil and Delitzsch read it as the permanent breach with his old life — Saul 'took him that day, and would let him go no more home to his father's house' — binding the verse forward into the covenant and the gift of arms that follow. The reformed Gill fixes the moment exactly: it was 'on that very day' he slew the Philistine, or as soon after as could be, that Saul took David not merely into his favour and service but into his household.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley draws the inference that gives the verse its weight: 'by which it appears, that before this David had not his constant residence at court.' Earlier David had come and gone — brought in to charm the king's melancholy with his harp (1 Sam. 16:21), yet still returning to feed his father's sheep at Bethlehem (1 Sam. 17:15). Now that coming and going ends. The shepherd who was fetched from the flock is kept at the palace for good.

The commentators do not divide here; they read one quiet act of providence. The same hand that anointed David in secret (1 Sam. 16:13) now advances him at court, so that, as Matthew Henry observed of the surrounding verses, David 'might by his preferment at court be prepared for future service.' Earlier Saul had already conceived a real affection for the young harpist and asked that he might 'stand before' him (1 Sam. 16:22); now that fondness hardens into a settled claim, and the visits become a residence. The retention is Saul's doing and God's ordering at once: Saul keeps near him the very man who will supplant him, and in keeping him schools him — for those who hope to rule must first learn to obey, and David, who had proved a dutiful son to Jesse, now proves a dutiful servant to the king. What looks like a king's favour is the training-ground of a king, and the verse that seems only to note a change of address in fact opens David's public life.
3:
The witnesses read the covenant of Jonathan and David as the formal seal of the love just described, and they agree on both its ground and its fruit. The grammatical-historical Ellicott finds its root in a shared faith: 'they were one in their God, in their faith, in their devotion to the Divine will.' Jonathan recognised in the young shepherd — who went out unarmed against the giant — the same trust that had once driven himself, with only his armour-bearer, against a Philistine garrison, when he said 'there is no restraint to the Lord to save by many or by few' (1 Sam. 14:6). That common confidence in the Invisible King, Ellicott argues, is what the covenant ratifies; and it held to the end, for when the friends parted for the last time in the wilderness of Ziph, the elder 'strengthened his hand in God' (1 Sam. 23:16).

Keil and Delitzsch keep the terms exact: Jonathan made a covenant of friendship with David 'because he loved him as his own soul,' the love being the whole motive of the bond. The reformed Gill dwells on its scope and its providential usefulness. It was a solemn agreement to maintain a cordial respect and to support each other's interest 'both in life and after death, whoever was the survivor'; and in consequence, Gill notes, David 'had a friend at court' when Saul turned against him — one who pleaded his cause, discovered his father's plots, and was the means of preserving his life.

There is no dispute among them, only a convergence: what began as spontaneous affection is now made binding, and God is quietly at work in it. Matthew Henry, on the same verse, marks that Jonathan 'endeavoured to perpetuate this friendship,' for true love 'desires to be constant,' and 'he that bears an honest mind startles not at assurances'; he draws the higher parallel that those who love Christ as their own souls 'will be willing to join themselves to him in an everlasting covenant.' The covenant that two young men swear in the flush of first friendship will, in the dark years ahead, become David's shelter — Jonathan's fidelity a standing rebuke to his father's treachery, and a proof that where the Lord unites hearts no jealousy of crown or succession can finally divide them. True love binds itself so, and here it binds itself the more firmly for being sworn.
4:
Jonathan seals the covenant by stripping himself of robe, garments, sword, bow, and girdle, and the witnesses read the gift as both a token of love and a window on an ancient custom. Keil and Delitzsch parse the wardrobe: the meil is the upper cloak, the maddim probably the armour-coat (the same word used of the harness David had declined, 1 Sam. 17:39), and the threefold 'even to' binds the several arms to the coat. For the act itself they point to the exchange of armour between Glaucus and Diomedes in Homer, a custom, they note, found also among the early Celts. Adam Clarke confirms the practice — presents of rich robes and of arms in token of friendship were frequent in the East and among the ancients.

Where a slight division opens is over the purpose of the gift. The reformed Gill takes it practically: the robe was given 'that David might appear at court not in the habit of a shepherd, but in that of a prince.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott records this suggestion — that it clothed the poorly-clad David fittingly — but judges it too narrow, for 'this kind of present was usual among friends in those remote ages'; he cites the Homeric exchange, where Glaucus gave gold armour for Diomedes' brass, 'a hundred beeves the shining purchase bought.' The gift, on his reading, is chiefly a rite of friendship, only incidentally a change of dress.

Underneath the small dispute the commentators are one: the prince disrobes to enrobe the shepherd, and the transfer of a king's son's own sword and mantle to the son of Jesse is a silent prophecy. Keil underlines that these were the very clothes and 'armour' Jonathan bore, the threefold 'even to' marking how completely he handed over sword, bow, and girdle together — nothing held back. Jonathan, who might have guarded his rank most jealously, gives away the very insignia of it; and the reader who remembers whose the crown will one day be sees in the gift a foreshadowing — the heir gladly investing the man who will reign in his stead, love outrunning every claim of birthright. As Matthew Henry noted of the scene, David is thus 'seen in Jonathan's clothes, that all may take notice he is a Jonathan's second self,' the two so knit that the one is content to be clothed in the other's honour.
5:
The witnesses trace here David's swift and blameless rise, and they fasten on the one Hebrew verb that governs the verse — he 'behaved wisely,' which carries at once the sense of prudence and of prosperity. Keil and Delitzsch render it 'acted wisely and prosperously' (the word of Joshua 1:8 and Deut. 29:9), so that whithersoever Saul sent David 'to battle' he succeeded; Saul in consequence set him over the men of war, and — the note they linger on — 'he pleased all the people, and the servants of Saul also,' the courtiers 'who are envious as a general rule.' Adam Clarke puts the promotion in blunt terms: Saul made him 'generalissimo,' what we would call a field-marshal. The reformed Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Wesley agree that the going out was upon military expeditions, a matter for which David was 'abundantly qualified.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the marvel the others only touch: the strange power David had 'to acquire over the hearts of men.' It was not only Saul and Jonathan he won, but his rivals at court and in the army, all of whom he was 'rapidly outstripping in the race for honour' — and yet he 'seems to have disarmed all jealousy,' his rise looked on with general favour. That last clause is heavy with dramatic irony, for the very universality of David's acceptance will presently become the seed of Saul's fear.

There is no debate; the schools read one portrait, which Ellicott says the narrator 'still farther' enlarges in the verses that follow. David's wisdom is not mere cleverness but a God-given competence that turns every errand to good account, so that success and modesty walk together in him. Gill adds that he was accepted not only by the common people who cordially loved him but even by 'Saul's servants,' who 'could not for shame but caress and compliment him' — the courtiers whom Keil calls envious 'as a general rule' disarmed against their own instinct. The man who could bear rapid honour without being lifted up is the same who acted prudently in the field; and the two graces — skill before enemies and lowliness among friends — are the credentials of the king-in-waiting, the outward sign that the Lord, who had departed from Saul, was now with David in all he set his hand to.
6:
The women of Israel come out with song and dance to welcome the returning army, and the witnesses set the scene with an eye to its music, its occasion, and its bitter sequel. Keil and Delitzsch note the textual questions some raise about the section but take the narrative straight: the women came from all the cities with timbrels and joy. The grammatical-historical Ellicott corrects a common misreading — 'when David was returned' points not to the homecoming after Goliath fell but to 'the close of the campaign which followed that event,' a series of victories that had for a time restored Israel's supremacy. The singing and dancing were the timbrel-dance, the women singing 'in the dance,' strophe answering antistrophe, as at the Red Sea and after Jephthah's victory (Exodus 15:20; Judges 11:34; compare David's own dancing later, 2 Sam. 6:14). Adam Clarke adds the instrument: the shalishim were three-stringed instruments, which he takes for sistrums of brass, jingling as they were shaken.

The puritan Matthew Henry turns the triumph into a warning. 'Now begin David's troubles, and they not only tread on the heels of his triumphs, but take rise from them, such is the vanity of that in this world which seems greatest.' The women, forward as ever to honour the victor, had a song whose burden ran that Saul slew his thousands and David his ten thousands — a comparison Henry thinks the poet made 'more just than wise,' setting David's achievement above the king's much as Moses distinguished the numbers of Ephraim and Manasseh (Deut. 33:17).

The commentators do not contend; together they hold two truths in one frame. The praise was in itself just — David, by felling Goliath, had in effect defeated the whole army — and yet the very acclaim that crowned him would arm his enemy. The reader is left to feel the peril hidden in popular applause: honour heaped on a modest man is a gift, but honour that measures him against a jealous king is a snare, and the song sung in the streets will echo, poisoned, in the palace.
7:
The refrain itself now sounds — 'Saul hath slain his thousands, and David his ten thousands' — and the witnesses weigh both its poetry and its poison. The reformed Gill hears the antiphon: the women 'answered one another as they played,' one company rehearsing a line and the other responding by turns. Adam Clarke guards against a wooden reading, for it was not literally true that Saul had slain thousands and David tens of thousands; better to render it that Saul had been opposed by thousands, David by tens of thousands — 'though Saul has been victorious in all his battles, yet he has not had such great odds against him as David.'

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the setting and the sequel. Ellicott identifies the couplet as 'the favourite refrain of an old national or folk-song,' one so well loved that the Philistines themselves would quote it back (1 Sam. 29:5). Keil and Delitzsch record what it worked in the king: the words displeased Saul, who said 'they have given David ten thousands, and to me thousands, and there is only the kingdom more for him.' Behind that jealous arithmetic, they add — citing Gerlach — lay 'a deep inward truth': if a shepherd boy must decide the victory that the king could not, this was one more mark of Saul's rejection.

Here a real difference of judgement surfaces, carried over from the previous verse. Was the song meant to belittle Saul? The puritan Matthew Henry thinks not: the poet 'used a poetic liberty, and intended not any invidious comparison,' and in any case it was below a prince to resent a reflection on his personal honour when the public glory was sincerely intended. Indeed, Henry urges, the women 'did but give David his due,' since in felling Goliath he had 'in effect' defeated the whole army. Saul, however, read it as a measured slight and a threat to his throne, and from that day, as Keil notes, was 'looking askance' at the man the song had crowned. The witnesses thus present the couplet two ways at once — innocent praise in the singer's mouth, treason in the hearer's ear — and let the tragedy turn, as tragedies do, not on the words but on the heart that receives them.
8:
Saul's wrath breaks out, and the witnesses probe what exactly the king could not bear. The reformed Gill offers two grievances: partly that the women styled him 'plain Saul, and not King Saul,' withholding his royal title, but 'chiefly because they attributed a greater number of slain to David than to him.' The offence, that is, was not only the comparison but the demotion implied in the diminished honour.

The puritan Matthew Henry weighs the king in the balance and finds him wanting. Saul 'ought to have considered' that the song referred only to the late action and meant no slight to his former exploits; that David, in killing Goliath, 'did in effect slay all the Philistines that were slain that day'; and that even had the poet intended a comparison, 'it was below the great mind of a prince to take notice of such a reflection.' Instead Saul 'was very wroth, and presently suspected some treasonable design': 'What can he have more but the kingdom?' From that suspicion, Henry says, he began to 'eye David as one he was jealous of.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the outburst as prophecy against the prophet's will. In Saul's foreboding question there was 'not only a conjecture which the result confirmed, but a deep inward truth' — for, as Gerlach had put it, if the king stood powerless before the enemy at so decisive an hour and a shepherd boy came and decided the victory, this was 'an additional mark of his rejection.' Years before, the king had heard from Samuel the divine sentence of his fall, and ever since had been on the watch for the successor destined by the Invisible King; this 'dread expectation of ruin and dethronement' had been a powerful factor in unhinging his mind. Now the crowd's idol stood before him, and the terrible thought formed: 'Was not this gifted shepherd boy — now the idol of the people — the future hope of Israel?' The commentators do not divide; they agree that Saul's own words, meant as an accusation, are in fact an unwitting confession — the rejected king naming, in the heat of envy, the very throne he knows he has forfeited.
9:
From this day, the text says, Saul 'eyed' David, and the witnesses linger on the sinister force of the single verb. The reformed Gill unfolds it: instead of the pleasant look due to a favourite for his gallant service, Saul now regarded him 'with an evil, spiteful, malicious, and envious eye,' diligently watching every motion for signs of disloyalty, and — as the Targum has it — 'laid wait for him,' setting the snares the following history records. Keil and Delitzsch supply the grammar behind the picture, noting that the verb is a denominative from the word for eye, meaning to look askance; the very form of the word bends the king's gaze sideways.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the moment as the hinge of Saul's whole tragedy. 'From the hour on which the king listened to the people's lilt in honour of the young hero, in Saul's distempered mind hate alternated with love.' He still longed in his heart for the presence of 'the only human being who could charm away his ever-increasing melancholia,' yet 'dreaded with a fierce jealousy' the growing influence of the man he had lifted from the sheepfolds. From here on, Ellicott observes with a note of grief, 'all our memories of Saul seem bound up with his life-long murderous pursuit of David.'

The voices do not contend; together they mark how envy first shows itself not in a blow but in a look. Gill presses the point that the look was not idle: Saul 'diligently watched and observed all his motions and actions,' laying wait for any pretext of disloyalty or treason, so that the following history — the javelin, the marriage-snare, the pursuit — is already latent in this first sidelong stare. Before the javelin flies, the eye has already turned; the settled inward grudge precedes the outward act. It is a study in the anatomy of jealousy — love and hatred contending in one breast, the sideways glance the first visible symptom of a soul that has lost the Spirit of the Lord and cannot endure the sight of the man who has it.
10:
The evil spirit falls on Saul, he 'prophesies' in his house, and the javelin is in his hand; the witnesses converge on the meaning of that word prophesied and divide over it. Keil and Delitzsch take it as raving: the evil spirit of God (the same of 1 Sam. 16:14) so seized him that he 'raved in his house'; the verb here 'does not mean to prophesy but to rave,' the usage founded on the ecstatic utterances that marked the true prophets (1 Sam. 10:5). The grammatical-historical Ellicott agrees and sharpens it: the madness has entered 'a new phase,' from the earlier dull melancholia — which Browning pictured in his poem 'Saul' — to a murderous jealousy; and he presses the grammar, that the Hithpael conjugation used here is 'never used by an Old Testament writer of real true prophecy,' which is always the Niphal, so that Saul's was 'but a bastard imitation.' The wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads it the same way: his 'fits of frenzy returned,' the fruit of envy, and he 'used uncouth gestures and signs, as the prophets often did.'

Against this stands the reading of the other wesleyan-arminian voice, Adam Clarke, who leans on the reflexive force of the Hithpael, which can mean to feign an action. On his view Saul 'pretended to be praying in his family, the better to conceal his murderous intentions and render David unsuspicious,' uttering broken imprecations — 'God preserve my life,' 'Destroy my enemies' — while David played, and then suddenly hurling the spear. Clarke thinks 'this view makes the whole case natural and plain.'

Whichever sense prevails, the schools agree on the tragedy displayed: the man once caught up among the prophets (a sign of God's Spirit) is now caught up by an evil spirit, and the counterfeit exposes the loss. David, meanwhile, 'played with his hand as at other times' — repaying attempted murder with music, and eluding the javelin, Keil notes, not once but twice. The scene is a terrible emblem of grace withdrawn: worship turned to violence, the harp answering the spear, and the anointed of the Lord serving the king who seeks his life.
11:
Saul casts the javelin, and the witnesses divide over whether he threw it or only brandished it, while agreeing on the fear that drove his arm. Keil and Delitzsch take the throw at face value: Saul said within himself, 'I will smite David even to the wall with it,' and the true reason lay deeper than madness — 'Saul was afraid of David, because the Spirit of Jehovah was with him, and had departed from Saul.' The wesleyan-arminian Wesley likewise reads a literal cast, done while Saul was 'quite under the power of that evil spirit,' and matched by a second such attempt later (1 Sam. 19:10).

The grammatical-historical Ellicott demurs on textual grounds. The Alexandrian Septuagint and the Chaldee render the verb 'lifted the javelin,' and he judges the probable sense to be 'brandished' or 'aimed,' reasoning that 'it is hardly credible that if he actually threw it, David would have trusted himself a second time in the king's chamber.' Adam Clarke stands aside from the dispute to gloss the weapon: the javelin was 'the emblem of regal authority,' kings being pictured with it at hand, and among the ancients spears were even 'worshipped as representatives of the gods' — so that the very sceptre of Saul's kingship becomes the instrument of his murderous rage.

The difference is real but narrow, and it does not touch the heart of the matter. Whether the spear left his hand or was only levelled at David, the witnesses are one that the deed sprang from fear, not courage — the fear of a man who knows the Lord has forsaken him and cannot bear the presence of the one the Lord has chosen. Keil observes that David did not on this account leave the court at once, whether because he took the attack for a passing fit of madness that would soon spend itself, or, more likely, out of a 'firm believing confidence' in the God who kept him. And they mark his response with quiet admiration: he 'avoided out of his presence twice,' neither striking back nor fleeing, but eluding the blow and staying on to serve. The javelin measures Saul's despair; the turning-aside measures David's patience.
12:
The narrator names the root of Saul's hostility, and the witnesses read it as the theological pivot of the whole chapter: 'Saul was afraid of David, because the LORD was with him, and was departed from Saul.' Keil and Delitzsch call it a stroke of 'deep psychological truth' — the fear 'arose from the consciousness that the Lord had departed from him,' a consciousness that forced itself involuntarily upon Saul and drove him to the mad attempt on David's life. The reformed Gill fills in the sense: the Lord was 'protecting and preserving, prospering and succeeding' David (the Targum reads 'the Word of the Lord was for his help'), and Saul feared that David would in turn take his life and seize the kingdom God had given him.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws out the paradox. Saul now, in effect, 'proclaimed war with David,' yet 'he really stood in awe of him, as Herod feared John' (Mark 6:20). Henry's memorable maxim follows: 'those are truly great and to be reverenced that have God with them'; and, more piercing still, 'the more wisely David behaved himself the more Saul feared him' — a fear that would deepen through the chapter. Men think the way to be feared is to bluster, Henry adds, but 'the way to be both feared and loved is to behave ourselves wisely.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott completes the portrait. David, still at court, took the outbursts for 'temporary insanity' and would not believe in enduring hatred from one so great; but Saul, 'conscious that his old vigour and ability were deserting him,' recognised in David 'the presence of a power he knew had once been his,' and so could not endure him near, dismissing him 'honourably from the Court' with a military charge. Henry presses the pastoral turn the verse yields: this awe of David is a backhanded testimony, for 'those are truly great and to be reverenced that have God with them,' and the way to be at once feared and loved is not to threaten but to 'behave ourselves wisely,' since 'wisdom makes the face to shine and commands respect.' The commentators do not divide; they agree that the verse turns entirely on the presence and absence of the Lord — the same divine withdrawal that hollows out Saul is the divine companionship that crowns David, and the king's terror is, at bottom, the recoil of a man who has lost God from the man who has found him.
13:
Saul removes David from his presence and makes him captain over a thousand, and the witnesses divide over whether the step was promotion or exile — while agreeing that God overruled it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read it, on its face, as advancement forced by success: David 'went out and came in before the people,' carrying out his enterprises so wisely and prosperously 'that the blessing of the Lord rested upon all he did,' and this very success 'compelled Saul to promote him.' Yet the promotion backfired, for 'his standing with the people increased with his promotion,' filling Saul with more dread.

The wesleyan-arminian voices see the darker motive behind the honour. Adam Clarke judges that Saul acted 'under pretense of doing him honour, when it was in effect only to rid himself of the object of his envy.' Wesley agrees and multiplies the reasons: Saul removed David from his presence 'because he feared lest David should find an opportunity to kill him'; because David's presence 'now made him more sad than ever his musick made him chearful'; and 'principally, that hereby he might expose him to the greatest hazards.' The reformed Gill reads both prudence and hatred in it — to keep David out of sight and out of reach, and safer from any supposed design.

The debate is real but the witnesses harmonise it in the end, as Matthew Henry did on the surrounding verses: Saul 'did impolitely,' for in banishing David to a country command 'he gave David an opportunity of ingratiating himself with the people.' Henry adds the double motive behind the move — to keep David 'from under his eye, because he hated the sight of him,' and so 'that he might not secure the interest of the courtiers' — yet notes that it did but let David 'preside in the business of his country, civil as well as military,' and give 'universal satisfaction.' The stroke meant to sideline the rival in fact enlarged him. What Saul designed as removal, God turned to preparation; the field command that was to expose David to Philistine spears instead schooled him in leadership and endeared him to the nation. The chapter's irony deepens: every device against David advances him.
14:
The verse is brief and its witnesses few, but they hold its two clauses tightly together: 'David behaved himself wisely in all his ways; and the LORD was with him.' The reformed Gill reads the first clause in its double sense, 'both in the court and in the camp, in whatsoever service he was employed'; and he notes that the Hebrew may equally be rendered 'prospered,' 'for, generally speaking, those that behave wisely succeed well.' Then he points to the clause that grounds it all: 'the reason of it follows' — the Lord was with him. The wisdom is not native cleverness but the fruit of the divine presence.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, treating the verse with the one before it, say the same in their own key: David 'behaved himself wisely in all his ways,' and 'the blessing of the Lord rested upon all he did.' The prosperity is uniform — 'in all his ways' — because its source is uniform; where the Lord is present, discretion and success walk together, and no department of David's life falls outside that companionship.

Gill draws the christological line the verse invites, seeing in David here 'a type of Christ.' The pattern is the same the New Testament will name of the greater Son of David, who 'increased in wisdom and stature, and in favour with God and man' (Luke 2:52), and of whom it was already said, when he was first commended to Saul, that 'the LORD is with him' (1 Sam. 16:18). The witnesses do not debate; they let the short verse make its single, weighty point, which the following verses will only confirm — that this uniform prospering is not David's achievement but God's gift. David's wisdom is a mirror of divine favour, and his unbroken success in the teeth of a king's enmity is the visible token of an invisible presence — the same presence that had departed from Saul now resting, in blessing, on the man he feared. What Saul lost when the Spirit left him, David carries in every path he treads.
15:
Saul watches David's very wisdom and is the more afraid, and the witnesses fasten on the strange logic of that fear. The reformed Gill states the plain sense: because David 'behaved himself very wisely,' Saul 'could get no advantage against him,' David succeeding and prospering in all his enterprises and growing 'more and more in favour with the people.' The excellence that would have reassured a healthy mind only alarmed a jealous one.

Adam Clarke puts it in a sentence: Saul 'was afraid of him' because 'he saw that, by his prudent conduct, he was every day gaining increasing influence.' The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch tie the verse to what follows, reading in Saul's fear the spring of the craftiness that will next devise the marriage-snare: seeing David behave so wisely, and finding 'all Israel and Judah' loving him because 'he went out and came in before them,' the king could not attack him openly, and so turned to plots that wore the face of favour. What could not be crushed by force must be caught by cunning.

The commentators do not divide; together they expose the tragic inversion at the heart of Saul's malady. Ordinarily a servant's wisdom commends him to his lord; here it condemns him, for it is proof to Saul that 'the LORD was with him' and against the king. As Matthew Henry observed of this very chapter, 'the more wisely David behaved himself the more Saul feared him,' a fear that recurs, he notes, at v.29 as here. It is the surest mark of a soul at enmity with God that it is threatened, not soothed, by goodness — that the light which should draw it only makes it recoil. Men think the way to be feared is to bluster and threaten; but David is dreaded for his very excellence, and the wiser and gentler he grows, the more the king's terror mounts. David can do nothing right in Saul's eyes precisely because he does everything well; and the king's deepening dread is the measure both of David's integrity and of his own rejection. Gill's plain gloss holds the whole irony in a phrase: because David 'behaved himself very wisely,' Saul 'could get no advantage against him' — and could not forgive him for it.
16:
Over against the fear of the one king stands the love of the whole nation: 'all Israel and Judah loved David.' The reformed Gill weighs the grammar, noting that 'the verb is singular,' which 'denotes that everyone of them loved him' — in all the tribes as well as in Judah his own — so complete was the esteem in which he was held, and so fully 'had he got the hearts and affections of the people.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott dwells on the distinct naming of 'Israel and Judah.' The separate mention of the two great later divisions 'seems to point to the fact that the compiler of the Books of Samuel lived after the final separation of the ten tribes from Judah and Benjamin, in the reign of Rehoboam.' Yet, he adds from other notices in the book, a marked distinction between north and south had begun to exist 'long anterior to the final disruption' (compare 1 Sam. 11:8; 1 Sam. 15:4). And whatever the date of the writing, the point about David stands: 'although he was of Judah, the future king was equally popular with the northern tribes.'

The witnesses do not contend; they read one telling contrast set deliberately against the preceding verse. The reason given for the nation's love is the same activity that stirred the king's dread — David 'went out and came in before them,' leading Israel to victory and dealing wisely in the people's affairs. Where Saul, in v.15, was 'afraid of him' for behaving wisely, the people for that same wisdom 'loved' him: one man's fear and a nation's affection spring from a single cause. What Saul saw as a threat, the people received as a blessing; the very public competence that isolated David at court endeared him in the country. And in that universal affection, spanning tribes that would one day tear apart, the witnesses glimpse the future: the man loved alike by Israel and Judah is the man who will one day unite them under a single throne, the shepherd already gathering, by love and not by force, the flock he is destined to lead.
17:
Saul offers his elder daughter Merab with pious words, and the witnesses unmask the murder hidden in the piety. Keil and Delitzsch lay bare the design: bound by his old promise to give his daughter to Goliath's conqueror, and pressed by the people's love for David, Saul makes the offer with words 'that sounded friendly and kind' — 'only be a brave man to me, and wage the wars of the Lord' — while privately reasoning, 'Let not mine hand be upon him, but let the hand of the Philistines be upon him.' The reformed Gill agrees that Saul spoke 'not in friendship and good will, but designing to lay a snare for him.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott traces the phrase 'the Lord's battles' to its noble source and shows how Saul abuses it. This was the feeling that inspired 'every patriotic Israelite'; David himself had spoken so of the giant who 'defied the ranks of the living God' (1 Sam. 17:26; 1 Sam. 17:47), and the same idea named that lost anthology, the 'Book of the Wars of the Lord' (Numbers 21:14). In Saul's mouth the holy formula becomes a lure toward death.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke sharpens the moral edge, borrowing from Calmet: the wars of the Hebrews, 'while conducted by the express orders of God, were truly the wars of the Lord; but when the spirit of worldly ambition and domination became mingled with them, they were no longer the wars of the Lord, but wars of lust and profanity.' By Clarke's measure Saul's proposed campaign is doubly corrupt, for the ambition mingled with it is not even conquest but murder. The witnesses do not divide; together they show a king cloaking a plot in the language of devotion, sending his intended victim to fight God's battles in the hope that God's enemies will do his own murderous work. It is the chapter's darkest irony yet — piety made the mask of malice — and the reader is warned that no words are so easily counterfeited as holy ones, nor any snare so plausible as one baited with a call to serve the Lord.
18:
David answers the offer with disarming humility — 'Who am I? and what is my life, or my father's family in Israel, that I should be son in law to the king?' — and the witnesses read both the heart and the grammar of the reply. The reformed Gill hears a man 'surprised at the offer, yet not refusing it, but expressing himself with great modesty and humility.' The wesleyan-arminian Wesley paraphrases the second clause: 'how little is my life worth, that by the exposing of that to some hazard, I should purchase a king's daughter!' — and, shrewdly, sees policy in the piety: David 'sheweth not only his humility, but also his wisdom, in discovering so deep a sense of his own meanness, that Saul might see how far he was from aspiring at the kingdom.'

The grammatical-historical commentators labour over the difficult phrase 'what is my life' (chayyay). Keil and Delitzsch reject the obvious 'what is my life,' since it will not square with the interrogative 'who,' and take the word to mean David's 'condition in life, the relation in which a person stands to others,' his 'father's family' including all his relations — so that neither personally, nor socially, nor by kindred was he fit to be the king's son-in-law. Ellicott canvasses the options: it may refer (a) to David's personal life, though that is already covered by 'Who am I?'; (b) to his condition in life, as Keil holds, 'but it is doubtful if the Hebrew word here used ever has this significance'; or (c) to his family, as Ewald and Lange render it — 'What are my folks or relations?' — which Ellicott judges 'undoubtedly the preferable meaning.'

The lexical debate is fine, not fierce, and it leaves the substance untouched: whether David pleads the lowness of his station or the obscurity of his kindred, he pleads the same thing — his own unworthiness. The witnesses agree that the answer is genuine self-abasement, the more striking in a man already anointed to the throne. He who knew himself the Lord's chosen king speaks like the least in Israel; and that union of a great destiny with a lowly self-estimate is, for the commentators, the very mark of the grace that was upon him.
19:
The promise is broken: when Merab should have been given to David, Saul married her instead to Adriel the Meholathite, and the witnesses read the act as of a piece with the king's whole unstable character. Keil and Delitzsch state it flatly — 'Saul did not keep his promise' — and note that these verses are omitted from the Septuagint, since Saul's first pledge came to nothing so far as David was concerned. The grammatical-historical Ellicott sees in it 'Saul's capricious wavering nature, so painfully prominent in the last part of his reign,' and offers the likely motives: a fresh fit of jealousy, or the large gifts the wealthy Adriel could offer for the princess's hand, such bride-price to the father being customary — a liberality the comparatively poor David could not match.

The witnesses do not pass over the grim sequel the marriage bred. Ellicott follows Merab's five sons to their end, 'hanged in the hill before the Lord' by the blood-revenge the Gibeonites exacted from the house of Saul (2 Sam. 21:9). The wesleyan-arminian Wesley is blunter still, identifying Adriel as the son of Barzillai (2 Sam. 21:8) and judging the whole business 'an act of great injustice; and accordingly this marriage was accursed by God, and the children begotten in it were, by God's appointment, cut off.'

There is no dispute among them, only a shared moral reading. Saul's faithlessness toward David is not merely a private slight but a breach that God will requite in Saul's own line; the daughter withheld from His anointed becomes the mother of a doomed house. Ellicott notes too that this whole episode of Merab (vv. 17-19) is 'entirely omitted by the LXX,' the translators apparently failing to see 'any reason for Saul's sudden change of purpose' — a difficulty the commentators resolve not by excising the verses but by reading them as of a piece with the king's diseased and wavering will. The reformed Gill marks simply that this fell out 'at the time when Merab should have been given.' The witnesses let the deferred promise stand as one more instance of Saul grasping to keep his crown by treachery, and losing, in the grasping, both his integrity and his sons.
20:
Michal, Saul's younger daughter, loves David, and the king is pleased — but the witnesses see the pleasure turn at once into a plot. The reformed Gill names the ordinary springs of her love: she fell for David 'because of the comeliness of his person, his gallant behaviour, his wise conduct, and the general esteem and reputation he was had in.' Keil and Delitzsch observe that Michal's love supplies what Merab's lacked — for the pretext of the earlier broken promise seems to have been that Merab 'had no love to David' — and record the king's cold calculation the moment he heard: 'the thing was quite right in his eyes,' for 'I will give her to him, that she may become a snare to him, and the hand of the Philistines may come upon him.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the same double motion: 'the love of the younger of the two royal princesses for her father's brilliant officer gave the unhappy king a fresh excuse to expose David's life to peril, while at the same time he appeared to be endeavouring to carry out an old formal promise.' A daughter's genuine affection becomes, in Saul's hands, a fresh instrument of his cunning.

The commentators do not divide; they mark the pathos of the scene. Keil notes that when Saul spoke to David of becoming his son-in-law 'in a second way,' David 'made no reply, because he had found out the fickleness of Saul' and 'put no further trust in his words'; the silence is itself a sign of how far the earlier betrayal over Merab had schooled him. Two loves meet here — Michal's, which is real, and Saul's professed favour, which is a trap — and David, once burned, receives the king's fair speech in wary silence. The verse thus holds together tenderness and treachery: the princess loves the shepherd for himself, and the father loves only the hope that her loving him will get him killed. What is warmest in the daughter is coldest calculation in the sire, and the same marriage is to the one a delight and to the other a device.
21:
Saul says it aloud — 'I will give him her, that she may be a snare to him' — and the witnesses read the naked plot together with the providence that will overturn it. The reformed Gill catches the reversal at once: Michal was meant to be 'the cause and occasion of his fall and ruin,' but 'instead of proving a snare to him, as he hoped, she was the means of his deliverance,' when she later saved David from the messengers Saul sent to slay him (1 Sam. 19:12). Adam Clarke spells out the mechanism of the trap: Saul 'had already determined the condition' — a hundred Philistines slain — supposing David would undertake it for love of Michal 'and that he must necessarily perish in the attempt.'

Keil and Delitzsch attend to the machinery of the narrative, explaining how Saul both offers his daughter directly and yet commissions his servants to broach it privately, and noting the phrase 'in the one of the twain' — David shall this day be the king's son-in-law 'in a second way.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott ventures a long shadow forward: is it not possible that this 'dark plot of Saul against a life once so dear to him,' known to David in later years, suggested to him the very means by which, in his own darkest hour, he got rid of Uriah at the siege of Rabbah (2 Sam. 11:15)?

The witnesses do not contend; they let the verse teach its double lesson. Saul's transparency is itself a kind of judgement — a king so consumed that he can name his own treachery, twice repeating within a single breath that Michal is to be 'a snare' and the 'hand of the Philistines' the means of David's fall. And yet the plot is already beaten before it begins, for the woman meant to be a snare will be a shield. The commentators find here the quiet sovereignty that runs through the whole chapter: every net Saul weaves for David, God unravels, and the daughter given to destroy him is kept to deliver him.
22:
Saul turns to indirection, commanding his servants to 'commune with David secretly' and press the marriage upon him, and the witnesses read the courtly manoeuvre. The reformed Gill explains why the king resorts to go-betweens: David, 'being ill used in the affair of his eldest daughter, did not listen to the proposals of Saul as to the youngest,' and 'therefore Saul took this method to bring him into them' — the servants to assure David of the king's 'real regard' and 'good intention towards him.'

Keil and Delitzsch, treating the surrounding verses, note how this reconciles a seeming discrepancy — that Saul is said both to offer his daughter himself and to employ his courtiers to talk of it 'privately.' The two are simply the same design pursued by both open and covert means: the king speaks the offer aloud, and his agents work quietly to make it stick, 'commune with David secretly' being the express order he gives them. The grammatical-historical Ellicott adds the telling colour, borrowing Lange's remark that in the servants' fluent discourse there is 'something of the flattering, conciliatory tone usual in such circles' — the practised smoothness of men who know how to carry a king's wish.

The witnesses do not divide; together they sketch the atmosphere of a corrupt court. The message the courtiers are told to carry is true in its words — 'Behold, the king hath delight in thee, and all his servants love thee; now therefore be the king's son in law' — and false in its aim, wrapping a deadly condition in the softest flattery. David is being managed, coaxed toward a bargain whose real terms are hidden; and the smoothness of the approach is itself a warning. Where Saul once hurled a javelin openly, he now works through whispered compliments and borrowed voices, and the reader learns that a snare baited with kindness is more dangerous than a spear thrown in rage. The king who could not kill David in a fit now hunts him by policy, and enlists his whole household in the chase. Gill notes the shift in David too: burned once over Merab, he 'did not listen' to the fresh proposals, so that Saul must resort to intermediaries — the very need for go-betweens a token of how far trust between king and captain had already broken down.
23:
The servants deliver their flattering message, and David answers from his poverty: 'Seemeth it to you a light thing to be a king's son in law, seeing that I am a poor man, and lightly esteemed?' The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the reply as perfect sincerity — 'poor,' that is, 'utterly unable to offer anything like a suitable dowry to the king,' for David 'could not possibly suppose that the king would give him his daughter without a considerable marriage portion.' The reformed Gill notes only that the servants spoke Saul's words plainly 'in the ears of David,' so that he might weigh them.

Ellicott, also of the grammatical-historical school, draws out David's self-understanding. He 'dwells upon this fact of his utter inability to give the expected costly offering,' and 'evidently attributes to his poverty and his successful rival's wealth his former disappointment in the case of Merab.' David 'looked upon himself as a mere successful soldier of fortune among the wealthy chiefs who surrounded Saul'; his father Jesse, though a headman in 'tiny Bethlehem,' was, set beside the elders who formed the court, 'a poor man.'

The witnesses do not divide; they read a moment of genuine lowliness that the plot will exploit. David's poverty is real and his modesty unfeigned — he measures himself soberly against the great men of the court and finds no means to purchase a princess, no wealth to rival the Adriel who had carried off Merab. That very humility is the opening Saul has been waiting for: the king will answer the plea of poverty not with money but with blood, naming a dowry David can pay only at the risk of his life. The reader sees the trap closing precisely on David's virtue, his honest sense of his own smallness turned into the lever of the king's cunning — the very modesty that adorns him becoming the handle by which Saul hopes to destroy him. And there is pathos in the self-portrait: the anointed of the Lord, destined for the throne, reckons himself among the landless soldiers of fortune at court, unable to purchase what a wealthier and worthier man had bought before him. He does not yet know that the King who chose him needs no dowry, and will secure the marriage by a stranger road than silver.
24:
The verse is a brief hinge — the servants report David's answer to Saul — but the witnesses see it as the moment the king seizes his opening. The reformed Gill summarises what was carried back: David's words were 'expressive of his unworthiness to be a king's son-in-law, and of his inability to bring a dowry suitable to her quality.' The plea of poverty, faithfully relayed, hands Saul exactly the pretext he needs.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the report through to its deadly consequence. 'When this answer was reported to the king, he sent word through his courtiers what the price was for which he would give him his daughter.' And the price was chosen with murder in view: 'he required no dowry' of the usual kind (see Genesis 34:12 for the custom of the bride-gift), 'but only a hundred foreskins of the Philistines' — the slaughter of a hundred enemies, with proof. Beneath the seeming waiver of a marriage portion lay the design 'that he should thus cause David to fall by the hand of the Philistines.'

The witnesses do not divide; they let the short verse do its work as the pivot of the plot. Keil stresses that David meant his plea in 'perfect sincerity,' unable to imagine a king giving his daughter without a considerable portion — which is exactly why Saul's answer is so cruel, since it turns David's honest modesty against him. David's humility had said he was too poor to buy a princess; Saul's answer, framed as generosity, asks not silver but blood, and blood to be won only in the jaws of the enemy. What passes for a concession — 'the king desireth not any dowry' — is in truth the sentence of death dressed as a favour. The reader watches the courtiers, unwitting or complicit, ferry the fatal terms between an honest man and a murderous king, and feels how smoothly a court can traffic in another's ruin. It is worth marking, with the commentators, that Saul asks 'no dowry' of the ordinary kind — no gift to the bride's father such as custom sanctioned (Genesis 34:12) — but substitutes a service that looks like patriotism and is meant to be a death-warrant. The form of generosity and the substance of murder meet in a single demand, and David, hearing only the honour offered, does not yet see the grave dug beneath it.
25:
Saul names his price — a hundred Philistine foreskins 'to be avenged of the king's enemies' — and the witnesses expose the treachery folded into it. Keil and Delitzsch state the hidden aim without softening: 'Saul thought to make David fall by the hand of the Philistines,' though David, suspecting no craft and loving Michal, 'was satisfied with Saul's demand.' The reformed Gill reads the demand as Saul's answer designed to 'remove' David's objection about the dowry.

Adam Clarke explains the grim token: David must 'slay one hundred Philistines' and bring their foreskins as proof 'not only that thou hast killed one hundred men, but that these are of the uncircumcised,' and he notes a similar custom surviving among the Abyssinians in Bruce's Travels. The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses further into Saul's motive, citing Wordsworth after Theodoret: why foreskins and not heads? Here 'is a sign of Saul's suspicious and malignant spirit.' Judging David by himself, Saul 'impiously suspects that David would go forth and destroy some of the Israelites' — as he himself desired to destroy David — and so requires the foreskins as proof that the slain 'were not Israelites.' Ellicott adds that Josephus, 'with a strange exaggeration, mentions 600 heads as the price of Michal.'

The witnesses do not divide; together they read the dowry as a portrait of Saul's soul. The condition is calculated for death — a hundred combats against a hostile nation — and its very form betrays the king, for a man who plots murder assumes his rival plots murder too, and demands a proof shaped by his own suspicion. Keil adds the detail that seals the irony: David would in the event deliver 'twice the price demanded,' so that the very extravagance of the demand became the measure of his success. Yet the reader already senses the outcome the plot has not reckoned with: the Lord who is with David will make the snare the very stage of his triumph, and the price meant to destroy him will only display the divine protection over his life. Ellicott's citation of Wordsworth catches the moral exactly — the demand for foreskins rather than heads is a self-portrait of 'Saul's suspicious and malignant spirit,' a man measuring his deliverer by his own murderous heart; and Josephus, the witnesses note, so magnified the tale that he made the price 'six hundred heads.'
26:
The terms please David well, and the witnesses read his ready acceptance against the savage colour of the age. The reformed Gill notes simply that the servants told David the king desired 'no other dowry than an hundred foreskins of the Philistines,' and the offer sat well with him. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley glosses the closing clause — 'the days were not expired' — as 'the time allowed by Saul to David for the execution of this exploit,' a set term within which the deed must be done.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott stands back to weigh the whole transaction morally. 'The king's design succeeded well,' he observes, for 'the prospect of the alliance with Saul spurred on this brave soldier to more daring achievements, and yet wilder feats of arms.' But he will not let the reader admire the scene uncritically: 'the savage, half-barbarous state of the age comes prominently into view when we reflect upon the ferocious cruelty of such an offer being made and accepted, and carried out with even more than the required number of victims.'

The witnesses do not divide; they hold the verse's two notes together — David's honest eagerness and the age's harshness. That David 'was pleased' shows how little he suspected the trap and how genuinely he prized the royal alliance and his love for Michal; that the bargain itself is a thing of blood shows the rough world in which the shepherd-king rose. And the clause 'the days were not expired,' Wesley's set term, lets the reader feel the pressure under which David acted — a limited season within which the perilous deed had to be done, so that Saul's design pressed him toward exactly the danger the king intended. The commentators neither excuse the brutality nor blame David for a valour employed against the acknowledged enemies of the living God; they leave the reader sobered, aware that even the honoured deeds of the Old Testament heroes were done in a violent day, and that God worked his purposes through, not apart from, the hard realities of the time. Ellicott's phrase lingers — the whole transaction, offered and accepted and overpaid, shows 'the savage, half-barbarous state of the age' — and yet it is on just such a stage, and no cleaner one, that the Lord is seen to be with David, guarding the life that Saul had staked on the enemy's spears.
27:
David not only meets the deadly condition but doubles it, and the witnesses divide over the number even as they marvel at the deed. Keil and Delitzsch follow the Hebrew: David 'slew of the Philistines two hundred men,' brought their foreskins 'in full tale to the king,' and so Saul was obliged to give him Michal. The reformed Gill reads the surplus as eloquent — the extra hundred done 'to show his regard to the orders of Saul, and his obedience to him, and to testify the sincerity of his affections to his daughter,' as well as 'his zeal for God and his country.' The wesleyan-arminian Wesley agrees that David 'doubled the number required, to oblige Saul the more to the performance of his promise, and to shew his great respect and affection to Saul's daughter.'

The other wesleyan-arminian voice, Adam Clarke, disputes the figure. The Septuagint has only a hundred; Saul had covenanted for a hundred; and David himself says he 'espoused Michal for a hundred' (2 Sam. 3:14); 'hence it is likely that one hundred is the true reading.' Gill, too, records that 'the Greek version has only one hundred.' So the witnesses set two readings side by side — the Masoretic two hundred, read as David's generous overplus, against the hundred of the Septuagint and the later reference, urged as the original number.

The dispute over the count does not touch what the verse chiefly shows. Whether one hundred or two, the snare has sprung shut on the schemer: the deed Saul designed for David's death has instead made him the king's son-in-law and covered him with honour. Gill reads the overplus as a many-sided testimony — of obedience to the king, of sincere affection to Michal, and of 'zeal for God and his country against their avowed enemies' — so that even the surplus blood preaches David's loyalty rather than his ambition. Keil marks the sequel that seals it — Saul 'saw and knew that the LORD was with David, and that Michal loved him' — so that the plot recoils, and the king who staked his daughter on David's ruin must instead hand her over as the trophy of David's triumph.
28:
Saul 'saw and knew that the LORD was with David,' and the witnesses read the recognition as the point where jealousy hardens into permanent enmity. The reformed Gill lists how Saul perceived it: 'by the favour he gave him among men, by overruling all the steps Saul took to do him hurt for his good, and in giving him success in all that he engaged in' — the Targum rendering it 'that the Word of the Lord was for the help of David.' The very failure of every plot became the proof that God stood with the intended victim.

Keil and Delitzsch trace the psychology of the moment. The knowledge that David 'had carried out all his enterprises with success had already filled the melancholy king with fear'; but when this latest scheme, meant to devote David 'to certain death,' also failed, 'the conviction was forced upon him that Jehovah was with David, and that he was miraculously protected by Him' — and this, joined to Michal's love, made his fear 'grow into a lifelong enmity,' his evil spirit urging him 'to greater and greater hardness of heart.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott paints the same picture: 'the success of the last savage enterprise, and the return of David with his ghastly spoils, filled the unhappy king with dismay,' so that 'surely another and a higher Power was helping him.'

The witnesses do not divide; they mark the terrible irony that the very sight of God's favour, which should have humbled Saul, instead inflamed him. Keil traces the downward spiral: his evil spirit 'urged him ever forward to greater and greater hardness of heart,' so that each fresh proof of the divine presence with David only deepened his rejection. To 'know that the LORD was with David' is knowledge that might have led to repentance or to reconciliation; in Saul it leads only to a settled hatred. The commentators leave the reader with the sobering truth that the plainest evidence of God's hand, met by an unyielding heart, does not soften but harden — and that Saul's growing fear is, at its root, a war not merely against David but against the God whose choice David bears.
29:
Saul grows 'yet the more afraid of David' and becomes 'David's enemy continually,' and the witnesses read the verse as the settling of that enmity into a fixed condition of the king's life. The reformed Gill names the twofold cause of the deepened fear: 'because the Lord was with him, and his wife loved him.' Saul saw that these two — divine favour and the affection of his own daughter — made him despair of ever accomplishing his designs; and he feared that 'this marriage, which he intended as the means of his ruin, would pave the way for his ascending the throne.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch point to what turned the inward dread into open and lasting hostility: 'the occasion for the practical manifestation of this enmity was the success of David in all his engagements with the Philistines.' As often as the enemy's princes went out to war, David 'acted more wisely and prosperously than all the servants of Saul,' so that his name was 'held in great honour' — and every fresh success fed the king's fixed hatred. The word the narrator uses, they note, marks not a passing fit but a continual state: Saul 'became David's enemy continually.'

The witnesses do not divide; they close on the tragedy the whole chapter has been tracing. Keil marks that this 'general remark' about the continual enmity is set down by the narrator precisely to 'prepare the way for the further history of Saul's conduct towards David' — the open persecution of the chapters to come. Saul's fear has now hardened past recall into lifelong enmity, and — this is the bitter irony they underline — the more he plots, the surer David's rise. The very marriage meant for ruin binds David to the royal house; the very battles meant to destroy him crown him with honour. Gill's insight stands as the chapter's epitaph: what Saul designed 'as the means of his ruin' God is making 'the way for his ascending the throne,' so that the king's every stroke against the Lord's anointed does but hasten the thing he dreads.
30:
The chapter closes with fresh war and David's growing renown, and the witnesses attend to the Philistine raid, the reason for it, and the honour it won him. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the sequel to Saul's enmity: 'as often as the princes of the Philistines went out' to war, David 'acted more wisely and prosperously than all the servants of Saul, so that his name was held in great honour' — a general remark that 'prepares the way for the further history of Saul's conduct towards David.' Ellicott, of the same school, judges the raid was 'probably to avenge the last raid of David' (the two hundred slain of v.27), and, citing Wordsworth after the Rabbis, suggests the Philistines were emboldened supposing their successful foe would claim the year's exemption from warfare that the Law granted a newly married man (Deut. 24:5).

The reformed Gill takes up that same Jewish tradition and turns it to David's credit: the Philistines, hearing of the marriage and knowing the exemption law, thought to catch Israel off guard; 'whereas David understood that law better than they,' knowing it 'referred not to a voluntary war, but to that which was the command of God.' The wesleyan-arminian voices are brief and agreed: Clarke notes that of this war 'we know no more than that David was more skillful and successful in it than any of the other officers of Saul,' and Wesley that the Philistines went forth 'provoked by their former losses, and especially by that act of David's.'

The witnesses do not divide; they let the verse round off the chapter's great theme. Saul's malice has issued only in David's greater glory: every new campaign, meant by the enemy to humble Israel and hoped by the king to consume his rival, becomes another stage on which David's wisdom shines and his name is 'much set by.' The shepherd fetched from the flock now stands honoured above all the king's servants, his renown spreading through the land — and the chapter that opened with a friend's love and a woman's song ends with the whole nation's esteem, the Lord quietly exalting the man whom Saul cannot bring down.
